DST500 · A History of Madness · Winter 2026
A Critical Zine · Mad Studies · DST500
From Residential School Syndrome
to Genocide Response
Reframing Indigenous Distress
DST500 · Winter 2026
Press next to open
Residential school building, Canada, c. early 20th century. National Centre for Truth and Reconciliation Archives.
I · Language & Diagnosis
What Language
Does to Suffering

The word "syndrome" does something specific. It takes a WOUND carved by government policy, by the forced removal of children, the erasure of language and land, and relocates this wound inside a body.

Residential School Syndrome, a diagnostic category that emerged from clinical efforts to acknowledge the harms of the Indian residential school system, does exactly this. In naming the suffering of survivors as a syndrome, it translates genocide into individual pathology. It offers care with one hand while skirting accountability with the other.

The problem is not in the person.
It is in the structure.

This zine argues that framing Indigenous suffering through diagnoses like Residential School Syndrome reproduces settler colonial logic by locating harm within Indigenous bodies rather than within the colonial systems that produced it.

What studying Mad Studies kept placing in front of me was a simple question: whose interests does a diagnosis actually serve?

DSM-V Extension · Clinical Reference Restricted
"Residential School Syndrome"
Diagnostic Criteria
Re-experiencing traumatic events related to residential school attendance
Persistent avoidance of stimuli associated with that experience
Negative alterations in cognition and mood
Duration: more than one month
Classification: Culture-Bound Extension of PTSD
This diagnostic extension translates collective experiences of forced assimilation, cultural erasure, and abuse into individualized psychiatric symptoms. The collective is made personal. The political is made clinical.
Who decided this
was a disorder?
Government of Canada · Health Canada · TRC Partnership
"Healing for Survivors"
The Government of Canada is committed to supporting the mental health of residential school survivors through evidence-based intervention:
—Individual counselling and trauma-focused therapy
—Crisis intervention, available 24/7
—Community healing programs grounded in evidence-based practice
—Cultural liaisons to "bridge" Indigenous and Western approaches
Notice what is missing:
Land. Sovereignty. Justice.
"Because we care
about your wellbeing."
II · Naming & The State
What if the
Name Changed?

In her chapter on Residential School Syndrome, Renee Linklater, a member of Rainy River First Nations, surfaces a reframing from one of the practitioners she interviews: what if, instead of labeling survivors as disordered victims, we understood their distress as a "genocide response" (Linklater, 2014, pp. 116–117)?

This is not a semantic adjustment. It is a political one.

When we call it a genocide response, we are saying the problem is out there, in the policies, the institutions. No amount of individual therapy resolves a structural crime.

Linklater draws attention to how psychiatric diagnoses often follow experiences of violence, and in doing so, medicalize structural injustice (2014, p. 117). The diagnosis mirrors the very logic it claims to address: it takes something collective and makes it personal.

Roland Chrisjohn, Onyota'a:ka scholar of the Haudenosaunee Confederacy, contends in "The Broken Indian" that pathologizing Indigenous communities obscures the moral and legal accountability of the settler state. Not because clinicians are malicious, but because the diagnostic framework is structurally incapable of seeing genocide (Chrisjohn et al., 2006, pp. 39–67).

Patient Intake Assessment
Name:  
Nation / Community:   (optional)
Date of Birth:  
Primary Presenting Concern
Residential school-related trauma
Secondary Concerns
☐ Substance use  ·  ☐ Depression  ·  ☐ Anxiety
Assessment Notes
Patient presents with symptoms consistent with Complex PTSD and Residential School Syndrome. Cultural background noted. Elder consultation possible as supplementary support.
Treatment Plan
Individual CBT  ·  Trauma-focused therapy  ·  Medication as indicated.
The nation is listed as optional.
The land is not listed at all.
Upper Canada Land Survey, 1792. Library and Archives Canada.
III · Hegemony & Care
The State Is
Not the Healer

What the intake assessment does not consider is that the same state offering therapeutic services is also the one that stole the land, severed the relationships, and built the schools.

Kirmayer et al. (2009) note that even well-intentioned psychiatric frameworks narrow complex colonial harms into individual clinical symptoms, sidestepping the collective, relational, and political dimensions of what survivors carry (p. 317). The trauma framework does not lie. It just looks away from the most important part.

Leanne Betasamosake Simpson, Michi Saagiig Nishnaabeg scholar and member of Alderville First Nation, argues that reconciliation politics and state-managed healing domesticate Indigenous resistance by absorbing it into the very institutions responsible for the harm (Simpson, 2017, chap. 1). Healing, for Simpson, cannot happen inside colonial structures that continue to dispossess Indigenous peoples of land and self-determination.

This is what Mad Studies calls hegemony: biomedical authority normalized as compassion, as science, as common sense, while Indigenous knowledge is quietly marked as supplementary.
Lake Ontario, north shore, Grimsby, Ontario. Blue hour, 2012.
Water in Nishnaabeg epistemology carries legal and relational meaning that treaty maps systematically erased.
Greg's Photography, 2012. Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 3.0.
IV · Three Nations, Three Practices
There is no single Indigenous approach to wellness, just as there is no single Indigenous people. What follows belongs to specific nations with distinct histories and should not be collapsed.
Michi Saagiig Nishnaabeg
Simpson (2017) documents healing through land-based practices: returning to the sugar bush, winter storytelling, Elder visits, and Nishnaabemowin immersion. Simpson calls such practices biskaabiiyang, returning to oneself through land and relation. These are not supplements to therapy (chap. 9).
Anishinaabe · Rainy River First Nations
Linklater's (2014) practitioners document Elder-guided Midewiwin ceremony, naming practices, and community stabilization networks that actively prevented psychiatric hospitalization. Not as supplements to treatment. As treatment itself (pp. 60–72).
Onyota'a:ka · Haudenosaunee Confederacy
Chrisjohn et al. (2006) argue the precondition for healing is structural accountability, not cultural programming. The Haudenosaunee Condolence Ceremony cannot function while colonial conditions remain intact. Healing begins with political reckoning, not a treatment plan (pp. 39–43).
"Healing, in his framing, begins with political reckoning,
not with a treatment plan."
DST500 · Winter 2026
Acer saccharum, sugar maple, Ontario. The sugar bush is not a symbol. It is a site of practice.
Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 3.0. Michi Saagiig Nishnaabeg territory, north shore Lake Ontario.
